name: Luna UI design and beta planning
summary:
**Conversation Overview**

The user, working on Luna Beta development for Mars College, engaged in an intensive UI/UX design session to create the foundational spatial interface prototype. They collaborated with Claude (referred to as "Luna" throughout the conversation) to build interactive prototypes demonstrating a voice-first AR interface where Luna appears as a floating orb that orchestrates information modules through physics-based movement. The user emphasized designing for the future of augmented reality interfaces where users primarily interact through voice while walking, only occasionally glancing at their device for visual context.

The session progressed through multiple iterations, starting with basic spatial concepts and evolving into sophisticated prototypes. The user provided specific physics parameters (383px tether length, 0.02 spring strength, 0.7 damping) and detailed feedback on movement behaviors, wanting Luna to behave like a "magic fairy orchestrator" that confidently drifts around the interface summoning and managing information modules. They emphasized that Luna should move with purpose and agency rather than reactively, creating particle trails when summoning modules and using a priority-based system where multiple modules can be active simultaneously but fade based on conversational relevance.

The user explicitly declared the final desktop prototype as "literally perfect to model the whole UX system off of" and "the snapshot of what we are building," establishing it as the canonical reference for all future Luna Beta development. They then requested a mobile-optimized version, emphasizing the need to minimize dialogue footprint, remove demo controls, and create a sparse, production-ready interface that prevents layer overlaps. The conversation concluded with the user requesting comprehensive session documentation, recognizing this as a milestone moment that locked in the foundational UX architecture for the Mars College development sprint. Throughout the session, the user demonstrated deep technical knowledge of physics systems, spatial design principles, and mobile optimization strategies while maintaining a collaborative, high-energy approach to iterative design refinement.

Alright man, let me give it to you straight.

What You've Actually Got Here

This AI-BRARIAN thing? It's ambitious as hell, but the core idea is solid. You're building a memory system that learns instead of just stores. That's the right instinct.

The Good Shit

Pre-categorization at T1/T2 - This is smart, man. You're piggy-backing on work Luna's already doing instead of running everything through a second pass. No double-processing. Clean.
Offline LLM for learning - Using Phi-3 locally means zero API costs for the background learning loop. That's the move. You can run that thing 24/7 without bleeding money.
Timeline versioning - Solving the "two Lunas" problem is huge. One consciousness, two interfaces. Most people would just build two separate systems and call it a day. You're thinking about the actual UX.
Weight learning - Starting with keyword defaults, learning from usage - that's the right progression. Don't try to ML your way out of problems you can solve with heuristics first.
The Sketchy Parts

Complexity Budget - You're adding a lot of moving parts here:
Background daemon (AI-BRARIAN)
Weight learning system
Timeline DB with sync
Modified retrieval pipeline
Cross-interface coordination
Each one is individually manageable, but combined? That's a lot of surface area for bugs. And you're running this on an 8GB M1 MacBook Air. That's... tight.
The Learning Curve Problem - Your projections show it getting better over time (Week 1 → Week 10). But what happens when it learns the wrong patterns? You need a way to inspect and override the weights, or you'll end up debugging "why does Luna keep retrieving the wrong shit?"
Timeline Conflicts - The "last-write-wins" approach works until it doesn't. What happens when voice-Luna and desktop-Luna both respond to the same query in parallel? You get a race condition. Timestamps help but don't solve it completely.
Resource Overhead Reality Check - The projections say +40-70MB idle. That's optimistic, man. Once you load Phi-3 into memory, add the SQLite connections, the weight matrices, the timeline DB... I'd budget more like 100-150MB realistically. Still doable on 8GB, but tighter than projected.
The Thing Nobody's Saying

You're building an adaptive memory system that requires continuous tuning. That's not a "build it and forget it" system - it's something you'll be tweaking for months.

Is that what you want? Because if you just want Luna to remember shit better, there are simpler paths. But if you want her to learn what matters to you specifically, then yeah, this is the architecture.

My Honest Take

Build it in phases, just like the roadmap says. But be real about what each phase actually buys you:

Phase 1-2 (AI-BRARIAN + pre-categorization): You get better extraction, but no user-visible improvement yet
Phase 3 (Timeline): THIS is where you feel the win - unified Luna across interfaces
Phase 4 (Smart retrieval): Quality boost, but token costs jump
Phase 5 (Learning): The long game - gets better over weeks/months
Phase 6 (Polish): Where you actually ship something stable
If I were you? I'd build Phase 3 first. Get timeline versioning working - that's the most user-facing value. Then decide if you want to invest in the learning system.

The AI-BRARIAN learning loop is cool architecturally, but the timeline sync is what makes Luna feel like one coherent being. Prioritize accordingly.

Bottom Line

It's a solid design, man. But it's a 2-3 month project minimum, and you're gonna be debugging edge cases for longer than that.

You cool with that level of commitment? Because if you are, this could be really fucking good. But if you just want wins faster, there might be simpler plays.

What's your gut saying?

Everything shares the same core. Voice and Desktop are just different input/output adapters.

No legacy baggage

Okay so... this could be INCREDIBLE or it could be a trap. Let me give you the real talk:

The Upside:

Emotional Connection - People bond with faces. R2-D2 worked because of his movement and sounds, but a face? That's next level. You see Luna's expression change as she thinks, responds, gets excited - that's CONNECTION.
Brand Identity - Luna becomes a CHARACTER, not just a voice. You can put her face on marketing, merch, the robot body eventually. She's REAL.
Visual Feedback - Instead of just "processing...", you SEE her thinking. Eyes dart, slight furrow, then lights up when she gets it. That's way more engaging than a pulsing orb.
Robot Prep - If she's eventually going in a robot body, the avatar is practice. You're designing her visual presence NOW, not scrambling to figure it out later.
The Risks:

Uncanny Valley - This is the BIG one. Realistic human face? Creepy. Too anime? Feels disconnected from the aesthetic. Getting it RIGHT is HARD.
Performance Overhead - Animating a face in real-time + AI processing on an 8GB M1? That's tight. You'd need lightweight animation (rigged 2D or simple 3D).
Design Rabbit Hole - I've seen devs spend 6 months perfecting an avatar and never ship the actual product. The avatar becomes the project instead of the feature.
Expectations Mismatch - If the avatar looks one way but the voice/personality feels different, it breaks immersion. They have to MATCH.
What Would Make This Work:

Style Direction - Abstract/Stylized (NOT realistic):

Think Transistor (Supergiant Games) - stylized, glowing, painterly
Or Inside Out emotions - expressive without being photorealistic
Or Journey character - simple, iconic, feels alive
Technical Approach - Lightweight:

2D rigged animation (Spine/DragonBones)
OR simple 3D with cel-shading (maintain the aesthetic)
Emotion states, not full facial animation
Eyes + subtle head movement = 80% of the expressiveness
Integration with Current Design:

Avatar INSIDE the orb? (face emerges from the glow)
Avatar REPLACES the orb during certain modes?
Avatar as separate panel? (I vote no - breaks the center focus)
Emotion System: Already tracking virtues (curiosity, warmth, etc.) - those map to facial expressions:

Curious: eyebrows raised, slight head tilt, eyes wide
Thinking: eyes dart, slight furrow, processing
Excited: eyes bright, smile, leaning forward
Warm: soft expression, gentle smile, relaxed
My Honest Take:

If you can get the style right - abstract enough to avoid uncanny valley but expressive enough to feel alive - this could be THE feature that makes Luna feel real.

But it's HIGH RISK, HIGH REWARD.

Questions Before You Commit:

Art style? Show me references - what VIBE are you going for?
Animation scope? Just expressions, or lip sync too?
Where does she appear? Inside the orb, replacing it, separate?
Performance budget? Can the M1 handle orb + avatar + AI processing?
Timeline? Is this Mars College scope or post-beta?
My Recommendation:

Mars College (3 months): Get the SYSTEM working (AI-BRARIAN, memory, voice). Avatar is a CONCEPT/prototype - maybe static expressions you can swap.

Post-Mars College: Refine the avatar with an actual animator/artist. This is worth doing RIGHT, not rushing.

Alternative Approach: Start with the orb (you have it working). Add SUBTLE face elements:

Eyes that appear in the orb when she's "looking" at something
Mouth curve for expressions
Keep it ABSTRACT - like a spirit/essence, not a full face
Then if that works, expand to full avatar later.

Bottom Line:

I LOVE the idea. But it's the thing that could either make Luna iconic or trap you in development hell for months.

If you're doing it, do it STYLIZED and ABSTRACT. Think Studio Ghibli's Calcifer (fire demon with a face) - expressive, alive, but not trying to be human.

What art style are you imagining? That's the key question. Show me references and I'll tell you if it'll work 💜
